title: TeuxDeux vs Things 3 for Minimalists
meta_description: For minimalists wanting a simple daily task list that resets without managing projects, areas, pick TeuxDeux when areas; Things 3 fails first in this matchup.
constraint_lens: You want a simple daily task list that resets without managing projects, areas, or layered review systems.

one_second_verdict.summary: Best for minimalists who want it simple.

verdict.summary: TeuxDeux wins for minimalists who want a clean daily list that rolls forward automatically. It centers on a calendar-style column where unfinished tasks move to the next day. Things 3 introduces areas, projects, and structured review layers that require extra decisions. If areas, projects, and review layers introduce extra structural decisions, Things 3 fails first.
verdict.decision_rule: If areas, projects, and review layers introduce extra structural decisions, Things 3 fails first.

sections.heading: Why TeuxDeux fits Minimalists better
sections.content: TeuxDeux fits this minimalist because it keeps the same friction from showing up in setup, daily use, and organization all at once.

bullets.point: TeuxDeux lowers setup friction in a practical way
bullets.why_it_matters: The user can get to useful task handling sooner.
Routine task actions take less thought and fewer steps.

bullets.point: TeuxDeux keeps the system easier to understand
bullets.why_it_matters: The structure supports the work instead of becoming extra work.

bullets.point: Things 3 offers more setup depth if the workflow grows into it
bullets.why_it_matters: The extra structure can become valuable later even if it feels heavy right now.

bullets.point: Things 3 can add more control to daily coordination
bullets.why_it_matters: That matters when the workflow truly needs stronger routing, views, or rules than the winner provides.

bullets.point: Things 3 handles broader organization once complexity is intentional
bullets.why_it_matters: The losing tool's extra layers are not useless, but they pay back only when scale and structure become real needs.

sections.heading: Where each tool can break down

items.fails_when: TeuxDeux becomes the wrong fit when the workflow grows beyond what a lighter task system can hold cleanly.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose Things 3 if the extra structure has become necessary instead of theoretical.

items.fails_when: Things 3 breaks down when its added layers keep showing up as friction during ordinary task use.
items.what_to_do_instead: Choose TeuxDeux when the lighter model is the real advantage.

sections.heading: When this verdict might flip
sections.content: This can flip if the deeper structure the loser provides becomes genuinely necessary instead of merely available. Then Things 3 may be worth the added complexity.

sections.heading: Quick decision rules
rules: Choose TeuxDeux if the main friction is too much structure too early.
Choose Things 3 if the extra depth is actually needed now.
Avoid Things 3 when the system keeps demanding more thought than the task does.

faqs.q: Which tool better matches this priority?
faqs.a: TeuxDeux fits this need better because TeuxDeux lowers setup friction in a practical way. Things 3 fails first when areas.

faqs.q: When should I choose Things 3 instead?
faqs.a: Choose Things 3 over TeuxDeux when the extra structure has become necessary instead of theoretical. Otherwise, TeuxDeux remains the better fit for this comparison.

faqs.q: What makes Things 3 fail first here?
faqs.a: Things 3 fails first here when areas. That is the point where TeuxDeux becomes the stronger pick.

faqs.q: Is this verdict only about one feature?
faqs.a: No. TeuxDeux beats Things 3 because TeuxDeux lowers setup friction in a practical way, while Things 3 loses once areas.
